events.text: In 1949, following defeat in the Chinese Civil War, the Kuomintang-led Nationalist government fled mainland China and relocated to Taiwan, establishing a rival government to the newly formed People's Republic of China (PRC).
events.short_text: In 1949, the Kuomintang relocated to Taiwan after losing the Chinese Civil War.

events.text: The PRC claims Taiwan as an inalienable part of China and considers its reunification a core goal of national rejuvenation. In a 2022 white paper, the Chinese Communist Party declared resolving the Taiwan issue as a historic mission, stating a preference for peaceful reunification but reserving the right to use force.
events.short_text: In 2022, the PRC reaffirmed reunification with Taiwan as a historic mission.

events.text: Between 2008 and 2016, under President Ma Ying-jeou of the Kuomintang (KMT), Taiwan pursued closer ties with mainland China under the '1992 Consensus,' which recognized 'one China' but with differing interpretations. While this policy expanded cross-strait exchanges, it sparked concerns within Taiwan about overdependence on the PRC.
events.short_text: Ma Ying-jeou supported the '1992 Consensus,' boosting ties but raising concerns.

events.text: After President Tsai Ing-wen of the Democratic Progressive Party declined to endorse the '1992 Consensus' in 2016, the PRC suspended official communications and responded with diplomatic isolation, economic sanctions, and increased military pressure against Taiwan.
events.short_text: PRC cut ties and escalated pressure after Tsai Ing-wen rejected the '1992 Consensus' in 2016.

events.text: In 2022, the PRC conducted military exercises near Taiwan, including live-fire drills and missile launches, further escalating tensions and prompting Taiwan to deploy its own military forces.
events.short_text: PRC conducted military exercises near Taiwan in 2022.
